topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias watched Clara from across the breakroom, a tight knot forming in his chest. The air around her seemed charged, almost aggressively cheerful, as she recounted her recent successes to the office grapevine. He meticulously straightened the stack of reports on his desk, the crisp paper a small, tangible shield against the acidic feeling churning inside him. When the announcement came—the promotion, the coveted senior role—a slow, deliberate exhale escaped him. He offered a smile that didn't quite reach his eyes, a practiced, polite curve of the lips. He made sure his nod toward Clara was just enough to acknowledge her achievement, a gesture devoid of genuine warmth. Later, he found himself lingering by the water cooler, deliberately slowing his pace, allowing his gaze to drift over her, cataloging the effortless way she commanded attention, a silent inventory of what he felt he was missing.

[story 2]

"Congratulations, Clara. Truly well-deserved," Elias said, his voice pitched a fraction too high, a brittle sound that barely masked the tension coiling beneath his skin. He clapped her on the shoulder, the contact brief and cool. Internally, he felt a sharp, unpleasant tightening in his jaw. He focused intensely on the condensation dripping down his glass of water, the rhythmic *drip, drip* a counterpoint to the frantic rhythm of his own thoughts. He took a long, slow sip, letting the silence stretch until it felt thick and uncomfortable. He made sure his next interaction with her was clipped, professional, every word measured and precise, a deliberate construction of distance between them. He noted the subtle shift in her posture—a slight relaxation, a settling into a new level of ease—and felt a distinct, unpleasant pressure behind his eyes.

[story 3]

I saw the email notification flash on my screen: Clara, promoted. A wave of heat rushed up my neck, making my skin prickle. I picked up my mug, the ceramic warm against my palms, and turned it slowly in my hands, examining the glaze as if searching for flaws. When Clara approached, radiating that effortless glow of victory, I managed a smile that felt stiff, like a poorly fitted mask. "That’s fantastic news, Clara," I managed, the words tasting like ash. I deliberately kept my eyes fixed on the corner of the desk, refusing to meet her gaze directly. I made sure my tone conveyed just the bare minimum of professional acknowledgment, a polite acknowledgment that held zero shared enthusiasm. As she spoke about her plans, I focused on the way my fingers gripped the handle of my mug, white-knuckled, a silent declaration of my own, unspoken dissatisfaction.
